environment: Coda /r/ immediately before /n/, /t/, or /r/ within the word (rn -> nn, rt -> nt, rVr -> nr with deletion of the Class I epenthetic vowel), including before the consonant-initial verbal elements -te (CAUS) and rusuy (DESID): r -> n. Pre-empts both the echo-vowel repair and the -h repair, so must be ordered before them. Does NOT apply across the converb boundary (koro teh, never *koh teh; Tangiku 1998: 225) nor, on present evidence, across plain word boundaries ('or ta). The pattern crosses a space only before the literal word rusuy.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Coda /r/: echo vowel or debuccalisation' (Chiri 1942: 472-473; Tangiku 1998: 225-227); morphophonology § 'Coda resurfacing and assimilation at the boundary' (Chiri 1942: 472) and § 'The West-coast -rV verb classes' (Tangiku 1998: 227); phonology § 'The liquid /r/' (Tangiku 1998: 227)
notes: Applies only to Class I stems (those with an r-final Hokkaido cognate: kor, nukar, 'inkar, mokor); Class II vowel-final stems never alternate (tura rusuy, not *tun rusuy; koore, mahnuure, 'eere). Exceptionless within its class across registers: West-coast elicitation (Tangiku 1998: 227), Asai Take narrative (ku=/e=/an=kon rusuy, konte; Murasaki 2001), East-coast L1 epistolary prose (Sentoku konte), 21st-c. revival prose (ku=nukan rusuy, Ainu Times). itah.ts instead applies the echo vowel to every coda r, predicting *korote for kor-te.

environment: Possessed (affiliative) suffix on monosyllabic -i/-u stems and glide-final (-y/-w) stems: Sakhalin generalises -he where Hokkaido uses -ye/-yehe (i-stems), a periphrasis or -p (u-stems), and -e/-ehe (glide stems). Mechanical only over morpheme-segmented input (V-ye -> V-he; glide + -e -> glide + -he). Polysyllabic -i/-u stems vary -hi/-hu ~ -he (generalisation in progress) and are NOT covered by the pattern.
source: morphophonology § 'Possessive allomorphy and the Sakhalin -he generalisation' (Chiri 1952: 463; Chiri 1942: 532, 1045, 1049; Murasaki 2024); prosody § 'Accent stability in the possessive paradigm' (Chiri 1942: 1049, 217, 469; Chiri 1952: 463)
notes: Exceptionless in the monosyllabic and glide-final classes (ni-he, ku-he, 'ay-he, haw-he); optional in polysyllables (kampihi ~ kampihe; conservative West-coast ku='itanki-hi, Murasaki 2024). ku 'bow' -> ku-he has no pairable Hokkaido form (the pages give only 'a periphrasis / -p'). Prosodic concomitant: Sakhalin keeps initial accent (rú-he) where Hokkaido shifts rightward (ru-é; Chiri 1942: 217). Requires hyphen-segmented input; the engine strips separators at the end.

environment: Coda /p t k/ standing immediately after /i/ and not followed by a vowel (a separator - or = is transparent to the vowel test): palatalises to [ç] and merges with the sibilant, surfacing as s (not h). Must be ordered before the general debuccalisation. The underlying stop resurfaces before a vowel (sik-i 'his eye').
source: syllable-and-coda § 'The after-/i/ rule: stop -> s' (Chiri 1942: 472) and § 'Hokkaido <-> Sakhalin coda correspondences'; morphophonology § 'Coda resurfacing and assimilation at the boundary' (sik -> sis; Chiri 1942: 472); orthography § 'Modern katakana and Cyrillic' (coda-h after /i/ absent in native vocabulary; cip -> cis, sik -> sis)
notes: The Hokkaido<->Sakhalin table also records a variant sik -> sis ~ sih 'eye' — an unexplained -h doublet for this one item. This rule is why /s/ is a permitted Sakhalin coda: much synchronic coda-s is neutralised post-/i/ obstruents. The grammar's examples are word-final; word-medial application (sittok -> sistoh) is an extrapolation inherited from itah.ts, which the grammar neither states nor contradicts. Taraika gate inherited from coda-stop retention (Itabashi 2001: 89; Tangiku 1998: 224).

environment: Hokkaido geminate stop (CC) or stop+stop cluster: the first half, being a non-prevocalic obstruent, undergoes the regular coda debuccalisation to h; the second half remains as onset of the following syllable (CC -> hC). Formally a sub-case of coda-stop-debuccalisation applied pre-consonantally; kept as a separate entry because the grammar states it as its own corollary. The pattern is subsumed by coda-stop-debuccalisation, so the two rules never conflict.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Geminates -> hC' (Itabashi 2001: 93); orthography § 'The coda-h: the orthographic shibboleth' ('okkayo -> 'ohkayo; Tangiku 1998: 2-2)
notes: East phonetics: hohke = [hɔxkjɛ]; word-internally the debuccalised coda is [x], or [ɸ] after /u/ (yuh-ke = Piłsudski júfḱe, root yup 'be strong'; Dal Corso 2024: 61). In itah.ts the effect is emergent from per-syllable application of the stop rule, not a distinct rule.

environment: Coda /p t k/ not followed by a vowel and not after /i/ (a separator - or = is transparent to the vowel test): debuccalises to the fricative x, written -h. Place-conditioned phonetic realisations: velar [x] after /a u o e/, palatal [ç] after /i/, labial [ɸ] after /u/ (Chiri 1942: 472). Strictly conditioned by the absence of a following vowel: the underlying stop resurfaces intact before a vowel-initial suffix/enclitic (mat-ak -> matax 'younger sister', itak-ahci 'they spoke', tek-ihi 'my hand', rap-us -> rapus, sik-i 'his eye').
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Coda neutralisation: /p t k/ -> x / -h' (Chiri 1942: 471; Tangiku 1998: 224), § 'Resurfacing before a vowel-initial morpheme' (Chiri 1942: 471; Dal Corso 2024: 61) and § 'Hokkaido <-> Sakhalin coda correspondences' (Itabashi 2001: 93); orthography § 'The coda-h: the orthographic shibboleth' (Tangiku 1998: 2-2; Kindaichi 1911 apud Sakaguchi 2021); dialects-and-sources § 'Diagnostic isoglosses' (Tangiku 1998: 2-2; Dal Corso 2025); morphophonology § 'Coda resurfacing and assimilation at the boundary' (Chiri 1942: 471)
notes: Dialect gate: Taraika / northern East coast retains p t k. West coast writes -h consistently; East coast (Piłsudski) usually leaves the coda unwritten, surfacing [x] before a plosive or before a vowel-initial word (itax ki, nax án; Dal Corso 2024: 61). The h:k particle correspondence nah : nak is this rule: nah 'so' has underlying /nak/, recoverable in the nax án sandhi. Chiri's word-internal spellings tex, max, kux, rax (Chiri 1942: 471) = word-final teh, mah, kuh (rah is never printed as such, only rax 'wing', so [rap, rax] is excluded from the mechanical examples).

environment: Coda /r/ before a following consonant other than /n t r/ within the word (i.e. in the same pre-consonantal environment where /p t k/ debuccalise): r -> h. This is the West-coast generalisation of debuccalisation to /r/; the East coast uses the echo vowel instead, so the rule is gated to the West baseline. The page's own triangulation hedges it: conditioning is 'primarily dialectal (East = echo-vowel, West = debuccalisation available), secondarily morphophonological (pre-/n t r/ /r/ is pre-empted by assimilation to n), and only residually free word-finally and before other obstruents' — yet its correspondence table gives the single unhedged output 'unarpe -> 'unahpe for the pre-consonantal case, which this rule follows as the deterministic West-baseline default. Must be ordered after coda-r-assimilation-to-n (which consumes r before n/t/r) and before the word-final echo-vowel rule. Does not cross spaces: word-final r before a consonant-initial word goes to the echo vowel (kapara tuki) or, dialectally, to -h (kapax tuki) — see coda-r-final-h-west.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Coda /r/: echo vowel or debuccalisation' (Chiri 1942: 472-473; Sakaguchi 2020: 43; Sakaguchi 2020b; Murasaki 2024) and § 'Hokkaido <-> Sakhalin coda correspondences' table; phonology § 'The liquid /r/' (Chiri 1942: 472); morphophonology § 'Coda resurfacing and assimilation at the boundary' (Chiri 1942: 472)
notes: Only one word-internal pre-consonantal pair ('unarpe -> 'unahpe) is printed in the six pages, so the example list stays at one — the other r -> h attestations ('utar -> 'utah; kapar tuki ~ kapax tuki) are word-final and live under the dialectal rule coda-r-final-h-west. East Sakhalin has categorical echo-vowel word-finally ('utara; Sakaguchi 2020: 43); its word-internal pre-consonantal treatment is unattested in the pages, hence the East gate. Taraika gated as conservative/unstated. itah.ts wrongly gives 'unarpe -> *unarape here. CAVEAT for the generator: because the residue is lexically diffusing, individual lexemes not printed in the pages may instead take the echo vowel word-internally (the converter's pirka -> pihka is a default prediction, not an attestation); kept regular because (i) the table row is unhedged, (ii) Sakhalin phonotactics forbid coda /r/ ('only /p t k c r ʔ/ are rerouted'), so a deterministic converter must reroute it, and (iii) -h is the only page-attested pre-obstruent outcome.

environment: Stem-final coda /r/ at the right edge of a free form (word-finally or before a space), when not captured by the n-assimilation or the West-coast pre-consonantal r -> h: an echo vowel copying the preceding root vowel is inserted, opening the syllable (rV). Before a vowel-initial element the /r/ simply resurfaces as an onset and no echo vowel is needed.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Coda /r/: echo vowel or debuccalisation' (Chiri 1942: 472-473; Tangiku 1998: 230, 227) and § 'Hokkaido <-> Sakhalin coda correspondences' (Itabashi 2001: 93); phonology § 'The liquid /r/' (Chiri 1942: 472; Tangiku 1998: 227); orthography § 'The coda-h: the orthographic shibboleth' table (Tangiku 1998; Dal Corso 2025)
notes: Categorical on the East coast (Piłsudski utara passim; Sakaguchi 2020: 43); on the West coast it competes with word-final r -> h ('utah, lexically diffusing — see coda-r-final-h-west), so the echo-vowel output is the pan-Sakhalin-safe prediction and is itself current in the West. The echo vowel is phonologically real, not orthographic padding: it deletes before rusuy/-te (kon rusuy, konte) and the resulting vowel-final stem takes the vowel-type collective =hci (koro-hci), never *=ahci (Tangiku 1998: 6, 230-231). itah.ts applies it per-syllable even word-internally, where the grammar requires r -> h or r -> n instead; here it is restricted to the right edge of a free form.

environment: Coda /s m n w y/ are the permitted Sakhalin codas and correspond to themselves (identity); only /p t k c r ʔ/ are rerouted. Identity rule: no pattern to apply; the engine's fall-through behaviour implements it.
source: orthography § 'The coda-h: the orthographic shibboleth' correspondence table ('-m, -n | -m, -n (kept)'; Tangiku 1998: 2-1); syllable-and-coda § 'The coda inventory' (Chiri 1942: 471; Itabashi 2001: 89) and § 'Coda /m/ and the sonorant codas'
notes: The pages state the identity as a table row but print no Hokkaido-vs-Sakhalin lexical pair for it (isam, pon, hum are cited only as Sakhalin forms), so examples is empty; the test instead asserts identity/idempotence on attested Sakhalin forms. itah.ts honours the identity for n/s/w/y but VIOLATES it for /m/ (unconditional m -> n), whereas the grammar keeps -m: isam -> isan is only a casual-speech West variant (see coda-m-nasal-assimilation).

environment: Word-final (and pre-pausal/sandhi) coda /r/ on the West coast: variably r -> h instead of the echo vowel ('utah beside 'utara). Lexically diffusing within the West; categorical echo vowel on the East coast. No mechanical rule selects between -h and the echo vowel word-finally within the West, so this rule is excluded from the deterministic pipeline; the pipeline's echo-vowel output remains a valid West form.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Coda /r/: echo vowel or debuccalisation' (Chiri 1942: 472-473; Sakaguchi 2020: 43; Sakaguchi 2020b; Murasaki 2024) and § 'Hokkaido <-> Sakhalin coda correspondences' table; orthography § 'The coda-h: the orthographic shibboleth'
notes: West Sakhalin: 'utah is the norm (Murasaki, FF-Ainu, Ainu Times); East Sakhalin: categorical echo-vowel 'utara, with debuccalised *utah unattested in the East subcorpus (Sakaguchi 2020: 43; Sakaguchi 2020b). Chiri's minimal doublet kapara tuki ~ kapax tuki (Chiri 1942: 473) and utara ~ utax on the same Piłsudski page show residual free variation word-finally; both 'utara and 'utah remain current in the West for this lexeme. kapax = kapah in Chiri's x-notation (x is his spelling of the debuccalised coda, house orthography h).

environment: Sakhalin-internal allophony, not a Hokkaido->Sakhalin orthographic correspondence: coda /m/ is variably realised [n] in casual West-coast speech, but is /m/ when carefully pronounced or before a vowel; before /k t n/ it is obligatorily [n] (or [ŋ]). Both coasts share the pre-/k t n/ assimilation. The orthographic correspondence table keeps -m, so the deterministic pipeline leaves coda m unchanged.
source: phonology § 'Nasals' (Chiri 1942: 472; Dal Corso 2024: 65); syllable-and-coda § 'Coda /m/ and the sonorant codas' (Chiri 1942: 472; Dal Corso 2024: 65); dialects-and-sources § 'The descriptive tradition' (Chiri 1942: 461: isam > isan 'especially on the West coast'); morphophonology § 'Coda resurfacing and assimilation at the boundary' (Dal Corso 2024: 65)
notes: Pairs are underlying -> surface within Sakhalin (East surface printed sánḱeta with palatalised k, prose [sankʲɛta]; 'sanketa' is its house-orthography normalisation), not Hokkaido -> Sakhalin: the orthographic correspondence keeps -m (Tangiku 1998: 2-1). itah.ts's unconditional coda m -> n encodes this casual/pre-consonantal variant as if it were the regular word-final correspondence — demoted here to sporadic, and the identity rule (coda-sonorant-and-s-identity) carries the regular claim instead.

environment: Debuccalised coda stop after /u/: phonetically labial [ɸ] (≈ Japanese fu); in the correspondence table and in some source spellings written f instead of h. Regular as allophony but notationally inconsistent across the grammar's own attestations (the same after-/u/ environment is also written -h: kut -> kuh; kana coda small ㇷ, kuh クㇷ), and no rule in the pages selects f vs h — so demoted to sporadic for mechanical purposes; the canonical house orthography writes -h and the pipeline outputs cup -> cuh.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Coda neutralisation: /p t k/ -> x / -h' ([ɸ] after /u/; Chiri 1942: 472) and § 'Hokkaido <-> Sakhalin coda correspondences' table (cup -> cuf 'sun, month', yuk -> yuf 'deer'; Itabashi 2001: 93); phonology § 'The fricatives /s/ and /h/' (cup -> cuf; Dal Corso 2024: 62); orthography § 'Modern katakana and Cyrillic' (kuh クㇷ)
notes: The two attestation sets (-f spellings cuf/yuf vs -h spellings kuh) are mechanically contradictory in the same environment; the pipeline follows the house -h. yup 'be strong' appears only as the bound root of yuh-ke (Piłsudski júfḱe; Dal Corso 2024: 61), so yupke -> yuhke is implied but not printed and is excluded.

environment: Hokkaido high-pitched (accented) open syllable <-> Sakhalin long vowel, written double (aa ee ii oo uu). Cleanest for open monosyllables; extends to disyllables whose accent falls on an open syllable. Sakhalin is mora-counting with a bimoraic minimal free form, so open monosyllables are systematically CVV. Requires Hokkaido pitch-accent data as input — not derivable from the segmental string alone, hence no mechanical pattern.
source: prosody § 'The pitch-to-length correspondence' (Itabashi 2001: 94-95; Hattori 1967; Hattori-Chiri 1960; Vovin 1993) and § 'Vowel length as the Sakhalin contrast' (Itabashi 2001: 88-89; Chiri 1942: 467); phonology § 'Vowel length' (Itabashi 2001: 88-89, 94); syllable-and-coda § 'Syllable types and the ban on clusters' (Itabashi 2001: 89); orthography § 'Long-vowel marking' (Itabashi 2001; Tamura 1996)
notes: DEMOTED from regular: linguistically regular given accent input, but non-mechanical over segmental strings, and attested mismatches block a one-to-one rule beyond accented open syllables: Hokkaido nina (LH) <-> Sakhalin niina (HL) 'grind', sini (LH) <-> siine (HL) 'rest' (Itabashi 2001: 95) — evidence Proto-Ainu had both length and pitch; sini -> siine also shifts the final vowel, unexplained in the pages. The always-long open monosyllables are listed only on the Sakhalin side (cii, nii, pee, ree, aa, too, poo, kuu, ruu, maa, nuu; Itabashi 2001: 89) — Hokkaido cognates are not printed for them, so no further pairs may be added. Near-pairs within Sakhalin: too 'lake' != to 'day', kuu 'drink' != ku= 1SG.

environment: Sakhalin-internal (pan-Ainu) possessed suffix -hV: in the regular cases the suffix vowel copies the final vowel of the stem — the synchronic reflex of Chiri's fossilised harmony classes A {a,u}, B {o}, C {e,i} (A+C and B+C may combine, A and B may not). Morphological: the rule ADDS a suffix, so it has no string pattern and is excluded from the segment-converter pipeline; examples are stem -> possessed form.
source: morphophonology § 'Possessive allomorphy and the Sakhalin -he generalisation' (Chiri 1942: 532) and § 'Fossilised vowel harmony in derivation' (Chiri 1952: 463)
notes: DEMOTED from regular: regular pan-Ainu morphology, but genuinely non-mechanical for a segment converter (suffix attachment, not segment correspondence) — listed for the etymon-correspondence generator. East coast may elide the h after a nasal-final noun — see east-h-elision.

environment: Sakhalin-internal collective/plural =(a)hci: =hci after a vowel-final surface stem (including Class I stems already carrying their epenthetic echo vowel), =ahci after a consonant. Rule ordering (Tangiku 1998: 231): (i) desiderative/causative attachment, (ii) vowel insertion, (iii) =(a)hci attachment. Morphological: adds a clitic, so no string pattern; excluded from the segment-converter pipeline; examples are stem -> collective form.
source: morphophonology § 'Causative and collective attachment' (Tangiku 1998: 231; Sentoku letters) and § 'Fossilised vowel harmony in derivation' closing caution; orthography § 'The coda-h: the orthographic shibboleth' (koro ; koro-hci, Tangiku 1998: 6); syllable-and-coda § 'Resurfacing before a vowel-initial morpheme' ('itah ; 'itak-ahci, Tangiku 1998: 224)
notes: DEMOTED from regular: regular morphology, but non-mechanical for a segment converter (clitic attachment). Diagnostic that the echo vowel is real (koro=hci, never *=ahci; Sentoku letters). Small lexically/idiolectally specified exception class of vowel-final stems with =ahci (konupurahci ~ yaykonopuruhci; Tangiku 1998: 231). East coast may elide the h after a monosyllabic open-syllable verb — see east-h-elision.

environment: Sakhalin-internal causative/transitiviser allomorphy: -re after a vowel, /y/, or /w/; -te after a consonant. Class I (underlyingly C-final) stems take -te with the r -> n change (kor+te -> konte); Class II stems already ending in -re show no alternation (mahnuure rusuy). Allomorph SELECTION requires knowing the stem class, so it is non-mechanical; the surface change itself is coda-r-assimilation-to-n, which is regular and mechanically tested there with the same pairs.
source: morphophonology § 'Causative and collective attachment' (Tangiku 1998: 229, 228; Chiri 1942: 472)
notes: DEMOTED from regular: the allomorph choice is morphology; its segmental reflex is asserted under coda-r-assimilation-to-n (these same example pairs do pass the pipeline). Konte attested across all registers including East-coast L1 free composition (Sentoku) — an active rule, not a memorised allomorph.

environment: East coast only, Sakhalin-internal: the /h/ of the collective -hci elides after a monosyllabic open-syllable verb (nu+hci -> [nut͡ɕi], not [nuxt͡ɕi]); the /h/ of the possessive -(V)hV may elide after a nasal-final noun, giving free variation (kotanu ~ kotanuhu). Pairs are underlying -> surface.
source: morphophonology § 'Causative and collective attachment' (nu-[h]ci -> nùcí; Dal Corso 2024: 65) and § 'Possessive allomorphy and the Sakhalin -he generalisation' (kotan-[h]u -> kotánu; Dal Corso 2024: 65)
notes: East-only, variable (free variation in the possessive case); excluded from the mechanical West-baseline converter.

environment: Taraika and the northern East coast generally retain the Hokkaido-style closed-syllable codas /p t k/; the coda-to-h merger does not apply there. Acts as the dialect gate (exclude: taraika) on coda-stop-debuccalisation, coda-stop-after-i-to-s and geminate-stop-to-hc; it has no pattern of its own.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'The Taraika exception' (Itabashi 2001: 89; Tangiku 1998: 224) and § 'The coda inventory' table ('Sakhalin: Taraika | p t k s … (as Hokkaido)'); orthography § 'The coda-h: the orthographic shibboleth' caveat (Tangiku 1998: 2-2); dialects-and-sources § 'The East-coast subgroup' (Murasaki 2024: 112)
notes: No Taraika lexical exemplars are printed in any of the six pages, so the gate is citation-backed but mechanically untestable from page-internal examples; the test only checks the gate mechanics (stops retained when dialect='taraika'). Taraika is the most conservative Sakhalin dialect, a witness to the pre-neutralisation state.

environment: Onset /r/ word-initially and after /n/ takes a retroflex, plosive-like quality, often sounding like t/d; after a nasal it may merge with t outright. Phonetic tendency on both coasts, registered by Piłsudski's notation and Chiri's description; not a systematic orthographic correspondence.
source: phonology § 'The liquid /r/' (Chiri 1942: 467; Dal Corso 2024: 64); dialects-and-sources § 'The descriptive tradition' (Chiri 1942: 461)
notes: Isolated lexical West/East variant hunara ~ hunana 'seek' (r ~ n alternation) is mentioned only in passing (orthography § 'Piłsudski's close-phonetic notation'); no rule derivable from it.

environment: Sakhalin-internal phonemic -> phonetic: long close vowels diphthongise (long ii = [iy], long uu = [uw]); /a e o/ lengthen cleanly to aa ee oo. Source of i~y / u~w spelling alternations across transcription traditions; orthographically the house transcription writes the double vowel, so the pipeline does nothing.
source: phonology § 'Vowel length' (Chiri 1942: 467: pū 'storehouse' = puw, cī = ciy); prosody § 'Vowel length as the Sakhalin contrast' (Chiri 1942: 467)
notes: Pairs are phonemic -> phonetic within Sakhalin, not Hokkaido -> Sakhalin. The 'to' field is a prose placeholder: there is no single orthographic replacement.

environment: Sakhalin-specific vowel-quality shifts in individual lexemes relative to Hokkaido, redistributing stems across Chiri's harmony classes. No phonological conditioning stated; for the etymon-correspondence generator only, never mechanical.
source: morphophonology § 'Fossilised vowel harmony in derivation' (Chiri 1952: 463)
notes: Lexeme-by-lexeme; no generalisable pattern.

environment: Lexeme-specific: the numeral 'five' asikne(p) undergoes medial cluster simplification (asikne- -> asne-) plus the regular coda p -> h. Single attested item; the p -> h component is regular, the syncope is not generalisable.
source: syllable-and-coda § 'Hokkaido <-> Sakhalin coda correspondences' (Murasaki 2009: 74); orthography § 'Modern katakana and Cyrillic' ('asneh < asiknep; Murasaki 2009: 74)
notes: Whole-word lexical substitution; the apostrophe (glottal onset) is written on the Sakhalin side per the grammar's convention.

environment: Single morpheme: the first-person plural exclusive object prefix is in- in East/West-central Sakhalin where Hokkaido has un-. Morphological u <-> i correspondence confined to one prefix; absent from the modern West coast, which has lost the inclusive/exclusive distinction altogether. Not a sound rule — listed for the etymon generator only.
source: dialects-and-sources § 'Diagnostic isoglosses' (Dal Corso 2025: 195; Satō 1985; Piłsudski 1912: 86; Sakaguchi 2021a: 124)
notes: One-morpheme correspondence, not a phonological rule.
